entries.title: Repeater overview - the main manual testing tool
entries.body:
# Burp Repeater - overview

**Repeater** is the manual testing workbench in Burp Suite. Its purpose is extremely simple and, at the same time, fundamental: take a single HTTP or WebSocket request, let you edit it by hand and send it **again**, as many times as you need. Each iterative send shows a fresh server response, and it is on this loop of "edit -> Send -> analyze response -> edit" that virtually any manual vulnerability research is built.

If **Proxy** captures traffic and **Intruder** automates fuzzing, then **Repeater** is the place where you *think*. This is where you bring a single interesting request and carefully tweak it, testing hypotheses one at a time.

## Why you need Repeater

- **Input validation (input-based vulnerabilities).** SQL injection, XSS, SSTI, path traversal, command injection - all of them are tested by inserting a payload into a parameter and observing the reaction. In **Repeater** you change one character, hit **Send** and immediately see the difference in the response.
- **Multi-step processes.** Cart -> checkout -> payment; password request -> reset token -> password change. **Repeater** lets you replay individual steps of such a chain without going through the whole scenario in the browser.
- **Confirming scanner findings.** **Burp Scanner** raises a signal, but an experienced hunter always reproduces the vulnerability by hand before writing a report. **Repeater** is the place where a finding is turned into a reliable, reproducible PoC.
- **Understanding application logic.** What happens if you remove a header? Swap an ID for someone else's? Change the method from `POST` to `GET`? **Repeater** answers these questions in seconds.
- **AI research (in the Professional edition).** Modern versions of Burp can plug AI capabilities directly into the request/response analysis process, helping you find anomalies faster.

## How a request gets into Repeater

The most common path is from the proxy history or from any other place in Burp:

1. Find an interesting request (for example, in **Proxy > HTTP history**, in **Scanner** results or in **Logger**).
2. Right-click it and choose **Send to Repeater**.
3. The hotkey is **Ctrl+R** (on macOS, **Cmd+R**).

The request opens in a new **Repeater** tab, ready to edit. You can also create an empty tab manually by clicking the new-tab button and choosing the type - **HTTP** or **WebSocket** - and then typing the request from scratch.

## The basic workflow loop

```
1. Send to Repeater  (Ctrl+R)  - the request arrives in a tab
2. Edit the request in the left panel (message editor)
3. Send                         - send it
4. Read the response in the right panel
5. Return to step 2             - next hypothesis
```

Each send is saved to the tab's history, so you can always go back to a previous version of the request and its response.

## Editions

**Repeater** is available in both **Community Edition** and **Professional**. The basic functionality (editing, sending, history, request groups, WebSocket) is present in both editions. Some advanced capabilities - for example, the AI assistant and custom action scripts for automatic request/response processing - belong to **Professional**.

## A tip from practice

Keep separate **Repeater** tabs in a single project for different "themes" of the test: one for an IDOR candidate, another for an XSS vector, a third for an authorization check. Do not dump everything into one tab: you will lose context and history. Grouping and renaming tabs (see the separate section) turns the chaos of twenty unnamed tabs into a manageable workflow.

entries.title: Working with HTTP requests: editing, Send, history, Inspector
entries.body:
# Working with HTTP requests in Repeater

Each HTTP tab in **Repeater** consists of two main panels: on the left, the **request editor**; on the right, the **response panel**. Between them is the **Send** button and the target indicator (host/port). All manual testing revolves around these elements.

## Editing a request

The request editor is an ordinary HTTP message text editor, but with Burp's syntax highlighting and conveniences. You can change anything: the method, the path, the protocol version, the headers, the body.

The message display modes are switched with tabs above the editor:

- **Pretty** - a neatly formatted view (decoded values, indentation in JSON/XML). Convenient to read and edit.
- **Raw** - the "raw" message byte for byte, exactly as it will go over the wire. Use it when precise control over the format matters (extra spaces, non-standard headers, parser bypass).
- **Hex** - a byte-level editor. Needed when you work with binary data or insert control characters (for example, a null byte `%00`).

Example: changing the method and a parameter to check for IDOR.

```http
GET /api/v1/orders/1024 HTTP/2
Host: shop.example.com
Cookie: session=eyJhbGciOi...
Accept: application/json
```

We change `1024` to `1025` (someone else's order), hit **Send** and look: if someone else's data comes back, we have found an IDOR.

### Undo / Redo

The standard **Ctrl+Z** (undo) and **Ctrl+Y** / **Ctrl+Shift+Z** (redo) work in the editor. This is a lifesaver when you accidentally break a request with an experiment and want to quickly roll back to a working state.

## Sending and reading the response

Click **Send** (or **Ctrl+Space** with the editor focused). The response will appear on the right. Burp will show useful metadata:

- the **response size** in bytes;
- the **response time** in milliseconds.

These two numbers are your main signals during manual testing. A jump in size often gives away a successful injection or a data leak; a jump in time gives away a blind vulnerability based on a delay (for example, `SLEEP()` in SQL).

The response panel also has **Pretty / Raw / Hex** modes, as well as:

- **Render** - render the response as a page (a rough HTML preview). Useful for a quick glance at how the result looks, but do not rely on it for JS-heavy pages.

## Tab history (back / forward)

Every send in **Repeater** is remembered. Above the request there are **<** and **>** buttons to move **back** and **forward** through the history of sent variations of this tab, and next to them is a drop-down list with numbered history items. This is invaluable: you can run five payload variations and then return with a single button to the one that gave an interesting response, and continue from there.

A typical technique: you send the basic "clean" request (the control response), then the request with the injection, and switch back and forth with the **<** / **>** arrows, comparing the sizes and bodies of the responses.

## Searching the response

At the bottom of the response panel there is a search box. Enter a substring (for example, the name of a reflected parameter, an injection marker or an error text) - Burp will highlight the matches. Indispensable on large responses: you instantly find whether your payload was reflected and in what context (HTML tag, attribute, JS string).

## Inspector

**Inspector** is a side panel that breaks the message down into convenient-to-edit parts so you do not have to fiddle in raw text. Right in **Repeater**, through **Inspector** you see and edit:

- **Request query parameters** - query-string parameters;
- **Body parameters** - body parameters;
- **Request cookies** - cookies;
- **Request headers** - request headers;
- **Response headers** - response headers (read-only).

The main benefit of **Inspector** is quickly editing a single value without the risk of breaking the request structure, plus built-in encoding/decoding. Select a value in **Inspector**, and Burp will suggest the applicable transformations (URL-decode, Base64, etc.). This is especially handy when a parameter is encoded multiple times.

## Target settings

Next to **Send** there is a target block: the **Host** and **Port** fields, the use-HTTPS flag, and the **Override SNI** option for manually substituting the SNI value during the TLS connection. Changing the target lets you send the same request to a different server without touching the `Host` header in the request itself - useful for tests like Host-header attacks and virtual hosting.

## Practical tips

- **A control request.** Always keep a "clean" version of the request in the history. Without a reference it is hard to understand what exactly changed in the response.
- **One parameter at a time.** Change one variable per send - otherwise you will not understand which edit produced the effect.
- **Watch the numbers.** Get used to looking at the size and time *before* you open the response body. Often the anomaly is already visible from them.
- **Pretty for reading, Raw for attacking.** Read in **Pretty**, but check the final formatting-sensitive payload in **Raw**.

entries.title: Sending request groups: in sequence and in parallel (single-packet attack)
entries.body:
# Request groups in Repeater: sequence and parallel

Sometimes a single request is not enough - you need to send **several related requests** in an organized way: strictly in order or, conversely, all at once. For this, **Repeater** has **tab groups** and group-send modes. This is a key tool for testing **race conditions**.

## How to assemble a group

1. Create a tab group and add the requests you need to it. Right-click a tab and choose **Add tab to group**, then the group you need (or create a new one).
2. For races it is convenient to multiply a single request: right-click the grouped tab -> **Duplicate tab**. You will get several identical requests in one group.

When the group is assembled, a drop-down arrow with group-send modes appears next to the **Send** button.

## Group send modes

Click the drop-down arrow next to **Send** and choose one of the modes, then click **Send group**:

- **Send group in sequence (single connection)** - all requests in the group go **one after another within a single connection**, after which the connection is closed. Suitable for client-side desync tests and reduces jitter for timing-sensitive attacks.
- **Send group in sequence (separate connections)** - a **new connection** is opened for each request: open, send, close, move on to the next tab in order.
- **Send group in parallel** - all requests are sent **simultaneously** with synchronization techniques applied (see below). This is the mode for race condition attacks.

You can stop a sequential send with the **Cancel** button on any tab.

## Parallel send and the single-packet attack

The most powerful thing here is the **Send group in parallel** mode. Burp itself chooses the synchronization technique depending on the protocol version:

- **HTTP/1** - the classic **last-byte synchronization**: several connections are opened, almost the entire request is sent over each, and the final byte is held back and then sent across all connections at once.
- **HTTP/2 (and newer)** - the **single-packet attack**: several requests are packed into **one TCP packet**. This practically eliminates the influence of network jitter, and the requests reach processing as close to each other in time as possible.

It is precisely the single-packet attack that turned the exploitation of remote races from a lottery into a reliable procedure: instead of fighting unpredictable network delays, you act as if you were launching all the requests "locally".

When the responses come back, the order of receipt is visible in the bottom-right corner of each tab (for example, `1/3`, `2/3`, `3/3`) - this helps you understand how the server processed the race.

> Note: requests that use macros cannot be sent in parallel - the macro would interfere with the synchronization.

## A bug hunting scenario: limit overrun

A classic race condition is bypassing a limit on a one-time action: apply a promo code twice, withdraw more funds than the balance holds, grab an item of which there is only one. The "check -> then change" logic is vulnerable if several requests slip past the check at the same time.

Step by step:

1. Find the request for the limited action, for example applying a promo code:

```http
POST /api/cart/apply-coupon HTTP/2
Host: shop.example.com
Cookie: session=eyJhbGciOi...
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 20

{"code":"WELCOME10"}
```

2. **Send to Repeater** (**Ctrl+R**).
3. Add the tab to a group: right-click -> **Add tab to group** -> new group.
4. Multiply the request: right-click the tab -> **Duplicate tab**, repeat a few more times. The group will end up with ~20 identical requests.
5. In the drop-down list at **Send**, choose **Send group in parallel** and click **Send group**.
6. Check the cart/balance: if the discount was applied several times, you have found a limit-overrun race condition.

## Tips

- **Aim for HTTP/2.** If the target supports HTTP/2, keep the requests on this protocol - the single-packet attack is far more stable than last-byte sync.
- **20-30 parallel requests** is a reasonable starting point. Too few and the race will not reproduce; too many and there is extra noise.
- **Do a control run.** First send a single request with an ordinary **Send** and make sure it is valid (correct session, valid code). Running deliberately broken requests is pointless.
- **Parallel is not just for money.** Races happen in email confirmation, 2FA activation, voting, invites, any counters. Anywhere there is a "check + state change", test a parallel send.
- **If the race is fragile** and is not caught from **Repeater**, switch to the Turbo Intruder extension - it gives finer control over timing and the number of requests.

entries.title: WebSocket messages in Repeater
entries.body: # WebSocket messages in Repeater **Repeater** can work not only with HTTP but also with **WebSocket**: manually sending, resending and editing individual messages inside a live WebSocket connection, observing the application's responses. This is indispensable when the application communicates with the server not via classic requests but via a stream of messages over `ws://` / `wss://` - for example, chats, trading terminals, real-time dashboards, multi-user interfaces. ## What a WebSocket tab consists of A WebSocket tab in **Repeater** contains: - a **message editor** - here is the message you are about to send, with the usual editor features (editing, search, display modes); - a **WebSocket connection** through which the message goes; - a **history table** showing all sent and received messages; messages you created manually are marked separately. ## How to send a message 1. Open a WebSocket message in **Repeater** (for example, via **Send to Repeater** from the WebSocket history in **Proxy**) or create a new WebSocket tab. 2. In the editor, change the message to match your hypothesis. 3. Choose the **direction**: send the message to the server (**To server**) or to the client (**To client**). An important detail: sending **to the client** is available only for connections that are still open through **Burp Proxy**. 4. Click **Send**. The message will go to the chosen side, and the reaction will appear in the history table. An example chat message into which we inject an XSS vector: ```json {"type":"chat","room":"general","message":""} ``` We send it **To server**: if another client (or a mirror in **Proxy**) renders the message and the alert fires, we have found XSS via WebSocket. An example of substituting the recipient to check authorization: ```json {"action":"getMessages","conversationId":"9001"} ``` We change `conversationId` to someone else's and see whether the server returns someone else's conversation (IDOR over WebSocket). ## Working with the history and Edit and resend The history table is a log of all the connection's traffic. To replay and refine any message from the history: 1. Right-click a message in the history. 2. Choose **Edit and resend**. 3. Edit it and send it again - repeat as much as you like, observing how different edits change the application's reaction. There is a handy checkbox for **automatically selecting the next received message** in the history table - it helps you not miss the server's response, which arrives asynchronously. ## Managing the connection A WebSocket is a long-lived connection, and **Repeater** lets you manage them: - **Connect/disconnect.** The toggle in the **WebSocket ID** header lets you break the connection or reconnect. - **Editing the connection.** Click the edit icon in the **WebSocket ID** header. From here you can **clone** or **attach to** any open connection, **create a new** connection or **reconnect** a closed one. - **Handshake manipulation.** When creating/editing a connection you get access to the **negotiation request** - that very HTTP handshake request that initiates the WebSocket. You can change it: headers, cookies, `Origin`, tokens. This opens up a separate class of tests - for example, checking for **cross-site WebSocket hijacking** (CSWSH), where the handshake is not tied to an anti-CSRF mechanism. ## Scenario: checking message authorization 1. Catch the application's WebSocket traffic in **Proxy**, find an action message. 2. **Send to Repeater**. 3. Substitute someone else's identifier (room, conversation, order) and send it **To server**. 4. If the server does not verify that the resource belongs to the current user, this is an access-control vulnerability. 5. Via **Edit and resend**, quickly iterate through several identifiers, confirming the scale of the problem. ## Tips - **Messages are often uncontrolled.** Developers carefully validate HTTP endpoints but forget about authorization and filtering in incoming WebSocket messages. This is fertile ground for IDOR, XSS and injections. - **Check both sides.** The ability to send **To client** (through a connection open in **Proxy**) lets you test the *client's* trust in data from the server - that is, DOM XSS on the front-end side. - **Do not forget about the handshake.** Many vulnerabilities (CSWSH, authentication bypass) live precisely in the handshake request, not in the messages themselves.

entries.title: Repeater tabs: groups, organization, notes
entries.body:
# Organizing tabs in Repeater

In a serious test you quickly accumulate dozens of **Repeater** tabs. Without discipline this turns into a mess of unnamed `Request 1`, `Request 2`, ... `Request 37`, in which it is impossible to find anything. **Repeater** gives you a set of tools to keep the workspace under control.

## Creating tabs

- Click the new-tab button and choose the type - **HTTP** or **WebSocket** - to create an empty tab and type the request in manually.
- Or send requests in from other places in Burp with the **Send to Repeater** command (**Ctrl+R**) - each one arrives in its own tab.

## Renaming

Double-click the tab title and enter a meaningful name. This is the simplest and most useful action for keeping order. Instead of `Request 12`, write `IDOR /orders/{id}` or `XSS search param` - an hour into the work you will thank yourself.

## Tab groups

Groups logically combine related requests (and they are also the basis for group sending and race condition tests):

- Right-click a tab and choose **Add tab to group**, then specify an existing group or create a new one.
- For grouped tabs, the **Duplicate tab** item is available (right-click) - it instantly creates a copy of the request in the same group. This is the workhorse when preparing a parallel send.

Group by meaning: "Authorization", "Payment", "File upload", "Race candidates". This way you switch between test themes rather than digging through a flat list.

## Notes

HTTP tabs have a **Notes** panel - a place for free-form notes on a specific tab. Record context there: "works only with a fresh CSRF token", "500 here on a quote - dig into SQLi", "needs the X-Trusted header". Notes are saved together with the project, and when you return to the test a day later you do not lose the thread of your reasoning.

## Tab display modes

When there are many tabs, the view toggle in the top-right corner of the tab panel helps you cope with them:

- **Scrolling view** - tabs in a single row with horizontal scrolling; next to it there is a drop-down list icon of all tabs for quick navigation.
- **Wrapped** - tabs wrap onto several rows, and you can see them all at once.

Choose wrapping when you want to see the entire "map" of the test at once, and scrolling when there are very many tabs.

## Closing and reopening tabs

The right-click menu on a tab and the close icon give you full control:

- the **close icon** by the title - close a single tab;
- **Close other tabs** - close all but the selected one;
- **Close tabs to the left** / **Close tabs to the right** - close the tabs to the left / right of the selected one;
- **Reopen closed tab** - bring back the last closed tab (a lifesaver if you closed the wrong one by mistake).

## An organization workflow (recommendation)

```
1. Send to Repeater (Ctrl+R)        - the request arrives
2. Double-click the title           - give it a meaningful name
3. Add tab to group                 - put it in a thematic group
4. In Notes, record context/hypothesis
5. As it grows - wrapped view, to see the whole picture
6. The excess - Close tabs to the right / Close other tabs
```

## Tips

- **Name + group = findability.** Two simple habits (renaming and grouping) save hours on large projects.
- **Notes are your journal.** Especially in bug bounty, where days pass between sessions: a note in a tab quickly brings you back into context.
- **A group = a template for a race.** If you see a request that smells of a race condition, immediately put it in a separate group - then with a single **Duplicate tab** you multiply it and send it with **Send group in parallel**.
- **Do not be afraid to close.** A closed tab can be brought back via **Reopen closed tab**, so clean up your workspace boldly.
